title: Apply: The Road to the Convention, "The Quartet," and the Push for Reform
description: Apply your understanding on the road to the Constitutional Convention and the push for reform in this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: The Road to Reform, the Constitutional Convention, and the Battle for Ratification
The Road to the Convention, "The Quartet," and the Push for Reform
Apply: The Road to the Convention, "The Quartet," and the Push for Reform

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from George Washington’s 1787  letter to James Madison.**

It gives me great pleasure to hear that there is a probability of a full representation of the States in [the Constitutional] Convention; but if the delegates come to it under [[☃ definition 1]], the [[☃ definition 2]] ends proposed will in my opinion be greatly embarrassed and [delayed], if not altogether defeated. I am anxious to know how this matter really is, as my wish is, that the Convention may adopt no [[☃ definition 3]], but probe the defects of the Constitution to the bottom, and provide radical cures; whether they are agreed to or not; a conduct like this, will stamp wisdom and dignity on the proceedings, and be looked to as a [[☃ definition 4]], which sooner or later will shed its influence.

**Which statement best summarizes Washington’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

choices.content: He worries that the delegates will want to completely change the Articles of Confederation, which he does not support.
He hopes that the Convention can identify the real flaws of the Articles of Confederation and address them with effective solutions.
He is certain that the Convention will be a failure, as any changes will be only temporary and not all states will attend.
He believes that the Convention will be successful, but it will lead to the creation of two new countries.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from George Washington’s 1787  letter to James Madison.**

It gives me great pleasure to hear that there is a probability of a full representation of the States in [the Constitutional] Convention; but if the delegates come to it under [[☃ definition 1]], the [[☃ definition 2]] ends proposed will in my opinion be greatly embarrassed and [delayed], if not altogether defeated. I am anxious to know how this matter really is, as my wish is, that the Convention may adopt no [[☃ definition 3]], but probe the defects of the Constitution to the bottom, and provide radical cures; whether they are agreed to or not; a conduct like this, will stamp wisdom and dignity on the proceedings, and be looked to as a [[☃ definition 4]], which sooner or later will shed its influence.

**According to the excerpt, what did Washington hope would happen at the Constitutional Convention?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A complete overhaul of the federal government
Some small changes to the federal government
No changes to the federal government
Two new countries with distinct federal governments

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from George Washington’s 1787 letter to Henry Knox.**

The legallity of this Convention I do not mean to discuss, nor how problematical the issue of it may be. That powers are [[☃ definition 1]], none can deny. Through what medium they are to be derived, will, like other matters, engage public attention. That which takes the shortest course to obtain them, will, in my opinion, under present circumstances, be found best. Otherwise, like a house on fire, whilst the most regular mode of extinguishing it is contended for, the building is reduced to ashes. 

My opinion of the energetic wants of the federal government are well known; publickly and privately I have declared it; and however constitutionally it may be for Congress to point out the defects of the [federal] System, I am strongly inclined to believe that it would not be found the most [[☃ definition 2]] channel for the recommendation, more especially the alterations, to flow, for reasons too obvious to [[☃ definition 3]].

**Which statement best summarizes Washington’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The people should wait for another method for fixing the Constitution, like allowing Congress to do so in its own time.
Although the Constitutional Convention is legal, the delegates will still need to convince their state legislatures to adopt the Constitution.
Although there are concerns about the lawfulness of the Constitutional Convention, it needs to happen to address the flaws of the current government.
The Constitutional Convention is not legal and any changes will not be accepted by the states.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from George Washington’s 1787 letter to Henry Knox.**

The legallity of this Convention I do not mean to discuss, nor how problematical the issue of it may be. __That powers are [[☃ definition 1]], none can deny.__ Through what medium they are to be derived, will, like other matters, engage public attention. That which takes the shortest course to obtain them, will, in my opinion, under present circumstances, be found best. Otherwise, like a house on fire, whilst the most regular mode of extinguishing it is contended for, the building is reduced to ashes. 

My opinion of the energetic wants of the federal government are well known; publickly and privately I have declared it; and however constitutionally it may be for Congress to point out the defects of the [federal] System, I am strongly inclined to believe that it would not be found the most [[☃ definition 2]] channel for the recommendation, more especially the alterations, to flow, for reasons too obvious to [[☃ definition 3]].

**Based on Washington’s argument, what is the meaning of the underlined sentence in the passage?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Constitutional Convention needs more powers to be able to alter the Articles of Confederation.
The state governments are denying the power of the delegates to make the Constitutional Convention legal.
The Constitutional Convention has all of the legal standing that it needs to alter the Articles of Confederation.
The federal government needs powers that it currently does not have under the Articles of Confederation.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from James Madison’s 1787 letter to George Washington.**

Having been lately led to revolve the subject which is to undergo the discussion of the Convention, and formed in my mind some outlines of a new system, I take the liberty of submitting them without apology, to your eye.

[[☃ definition 1]] that an individual independence of the states is utterly [[☃ definition 2]] with their [[☃ definition 3]]; and that a consolidation of the whole into one simple republic would be as [[☃ definition 4]] as it is unattainable, I have sought for some middle ground, which may at once support a due supremacy of the national authority, and not exclude the local authorities wherever they can be subordinately useful.

**What solution did Madison believe would be the most effective in addressing the need to balance the “individual independence of the states” with a collective authority?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Giving the states more power and establishing a loose alliance
Creating a strong central government with representation from the states
Unifying the powers of the state and federal governments under a single executive
Dividing the country into two separate countries to address its diverse needs

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from James Madison’s 1787 letter to Edmund Randolph, written a month before the Constitutional Convention. Madison wrote several letters to influential people, calling for changes to the federal government.**

I hold it for a fundamental point, that an individual independence of the states is utterly [[☃ definition 1]] with the idea of an [[☃ definition 2]]. I think, at the same time, that a consolidation of the states into one simple republic is not less unattainable than it would be [[☃ definition 3]]. Let it be tried, then, whether any middle ground can be taken, which will at once support a due supremacy of the national authority, and leave in force the local authorities so far as they can be subordinately useful. . . 

**According to the passage, what must a new American constitution include?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A national government that is divided into rival and hostile branches
A national government that is dependent on the state governments
A national government that has authority over state governments
A national government that has equal representation in the legislature

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from George Washington’s 1786 letter to John Jay.**

Your sentiments, that our affairs are drawing rapidly to a crisis, accord with my own. . . Experience has taught us, that men will not adopt and carry into execution measures the best calculated for their own good, without the intervention of a [[☃ definition 1]] power. I do not conceive we can exist long as a nation without having lodged some where a power, which will [[☃ definition 2]]  the whole Union in as energetic a manner as the authority of the State governments extends over the several States.

To be fearful of investing Congress, constituted as that body is, with ample authorities for national purposes, appears to me the very climax of popular absurdity and madness. . . . Be that as it may, [[☃ definition 3]] are a perfect [[☃ definition 4]] where thirteen [[☃ definition 5]], independent, disunited States are in the habit of discussing and refusing [[☃ definition 7]] with them at their option. Requisitions are actually little better than a [[☃ definition 6]] and a by-word throughout the land. 

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: official orders or requests for funds
the act of obeying a rule or law

choices.content: The current government is not working, as Congress does not have the power to enforce national measures.
States need to have more power than the federal government to combat unreasonable requests.
The Articles of Confederation invests too much power in the federal government, as it can act without consent from the states.
States need to create individual militias to keep order in case any rebellions emerge.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from George Washington’s 1786 letter to John Jay.**

Your sentiments, that our affairs are drawing rapidly to a crisis, accord with my own. . . Experience has taught us, that men will not adopt and carry into execution measures the best calculated for their own good, without the intervention of a [[☃ definition 1]] power. I do not conceive we can exist long as a nation without having lodged some where a power, which will [[☃ definition 2]]  the whole Union in as energetic a manner as the authority of the State governments extends over the several States.

To be fearful of investing Congress, constituted as that body is, with ample authorities for national purposes, appears to me the very climax of popular absurdity and madness. . . . Be that as it may, [[☃ definition 3]] are a perfect [[☃ definition 4]] where thirteen [[☃ definition 5]], independent, disunited States are in the habit of discussing and refusing [[☃ definition 7]] with them at their option. Requisitions are actually little better than a [[☃ definition 6]] and a by-word throughout the land. 

**Which weakness of the Articles of Confederation does Washington highlight in the second paragraph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: official orders or requests for funds
the act of obeying a rule or law

choices.content: Need for unanimous approval for amendments
Lack of national currency, as states developed their own
No national court system to protect citizens’ rights
Inability to raise money by collecting taxes

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from Alexander Hamilton’s 1783 letter to John Jay.**

We have now happily concluded the great work of independence, but much remains to be done to reach the fruits of it. Our prospects are not flattering. Every day proves the [[☃ definition 1]] of the present confederation, yet the common danger being removed, we are [[☃ definition 2]] instead of advancing in a [[☃ definition 3]] to amend its defects. The road to popularity in each state is to inspire jealousies of the power of Congress, though nothing can be more apparent than that [[☃ definition 5]] have no power; and that for the want of it, the resources of the country during the war could not be drawn out, and we at this moment experience all the mischiefs of a bankrupt and ruined credit. It is to be hoped that when prejudice and [[☃ definition 4]] have run themselves out of breath we may return to reason and correct our errors.

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: failure to produce the desired effect

choices.content: The American Revolution has left the government of the United States in debt, and the states need to take powers from Congress to act quickly.
The Articles of Confederation have created a government that can win the American Revolution and guarantee independence.
The United States is in danger of  financial collapse because Congress is too weak.
The United States is losing the war because the government cannot compete financially with Great Britain.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from Alexander Hamilton’s 1783 letter to John Jay.**

We have now happily concluded the great work of independence, but much remains to be done to reach the fruits of it. Our prospects are not flattering. Every day proves the [[☃ definition 1]] of the present confederation, yet the common danger being removed, we are [[☃ definition 2]] instead of advancing in a [[☃ definition 3]] to amend its defects. The road to popularity in each state is to inspire jealousies of the power of Congress, though nothing can be more apparent than that [[☃ definition 5]] have no power; and that for the want of it, the resources of the country during the war could not be drawn out, and we at this moment experience all the mischiefs of a bankrupt and ruined credit. It is to be hoped that when prejudice and [[☃ definition 4]] have run themselves out of breath we may return to reason and correct our errors.

**According to Hamilton, what is the problem with the government under the Articles of Confederation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: failure to produce the desired effect

choices.content: The states do not have enough resources to recover after the Revolutionary War.
The federal government is too strong, leading it to be unpopular with the states.
The federal government cannot address the military threats facing the nation.
The states have too much power and cannot solve national problems.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from George Washington’s 1783 letter to Benjamin Harrison.**

If the powers of congress are not enlarged and made [[☃ definition 1]] to all general purposes, that the blood which has been spilt, the expense that has been incurred, and the distress which has been felt will [[☃ definition 2]] in nothing, and that the band, already too weak, which holds us together will soon be broken when anarchy and confusion must [[☃ definition 3]]. 

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: If Congress does not give up some of their powers, the states will rebel.
If Congress does not get more power, the nation will fall apart.
If Congress does not raise more money, the nation cannot pay back its debts.
If Congress does not secure its borders, the nation will face another war.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from George Washington’s 1783 letter to Benjamin Harrison.**

If the powers of congress are not enlarged and made [[☃ definition 1]] to all general purposes, that the blood which has been spilt, the expense that has been incurred, and the distress which has been felt will [[☃ definition 2]] in nothing, and that the band, already too weak, which holds us together will soon be broken when anarchy and confusion must [[☃ definition 3]]. 

**Which document is Washington critiquing in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Articles of Confederation
The Declaration of Independence
The Northwest Ordinance
The Constitution
